title: Chapter Four: The Previous
content: I don't sleep.

I lie in the dark and I think about Peter. Peter Quint, according to the TikTok caption. Peter, who is dead, according to every searchable record. Peter, who says hello, according to a seven-year-old girl who smiled like she was handing me a gift.

Two days. Two days at Bly and the twins have invoked two dead people. Day one: the woman in the east wing window. Day two: Peter says hello. At this rate, day three will feature a full seance in the breakfast nook.

The ceiling above my bed has water stains shaped like continents. I've been mapping them. Here is Europe. Here is a landmass that doesn't exist. My eyes trace the borders again and again until the shapes lose meaning and become just discoloration, just moisture, just a house decomposing slowly around me.

The radiator clicks. Old houses do this. They settle. They talk to themselves. I know this. I grew up in a house built in 1906 that groaned like a living thing every winter. This is not that. This is me lying in a bed that smells like lavender and something underneath the lavender, something chemical, and thinking: *Peter says hello.*

Who says hello? Dead people don't say hello. Living people pretending to be dead people say hello. Children who've found a narrative and are running with it say hello. Or — and I don't like this option, I don't like it at all — something at Bly says hello, and the children are the translators.
I sit up. The clock on the nightstand reads 2:47 AM. The sheets are damp. Mine or the bed's, I can't tell.
I can't stay here.

I pull on my jumper — the thick one, the one that still smells like my apartment in London, like cheap coffee and the fabric softener I've used since university — and I slide my feet into my shoes. I don't turn on the lamp. The hallway light from the corridor seeps under the door, enough to navigate by.

The door opens with a sound like a held breath releasing. The corridor is empty. Of course it's empty. What did I expect — a figure at the end of the hall, backlit, swaying? This isn't a film. This is a house. Houses have corridors. Corridors are empty at 2:47 AM. This is all very normal and I am being very normal about it.

I go left, away from the children's wing. Toward the servants' corridor, the part of the tour Mrs. Grose skipped. She'd walked me past it — "That's the old services area, not much to see" — and I'd noted the quickened pace, the way her keys jingled faster as she herded me along. The servant areas are always where the stories are. The upstairs people get the nice furniture. The downstairs people get the truth.

The floor changes from hardwood to tile. Colder through the soles of my shoes. The air changes too — cooler, damper, carrying the faint sour smell of old stone and something else. Bleach, maybe. Recently applied bleach, fighting a losing battle against the centuries.
Doors line the corridor. Most are closed. I try the first: locked. The second: a linen closet, sheets stacked with military precision. The third: locked. The fourth gives under my hand.
A storage closet. Small. No window. I pull out my phone and use the flashlight.
The suitcase sits on a shelf at eye level, half-open. Black. Medium-sized. A luggage tag still attached: *J. Jessel, Heathrow → Inverness.* The sticker from the airline is dated fourteen months ago.
Miss Jessel. The previous. The one who found the position wasn't to her liking.
I reach for it. My hand hesitates. This is someone's belongings. This is a violation. This is exactly what I came looking for. I open it wider.

Clothes, neatly folded. A toiletry bag with the zipper half-closed, a tube of toothpaste leaking mint-scented paste onto the nylon lining. A pair of shoes placed carefully at the bottom, heels worn down on the insides — she walked with her feet turned slightly inward. A woman who existed. A woman who packed as if she were coming back.
Beneath the clothes: a book. Plain cover, no title on the spine. A journal.

I pick it up. It falls open in my hands, as if it's been opened to this page a hundred times. The spine is cracked there. The handwriting is neat at first — practiced, the hand of someone trained in cursive, who looped her Gs and crossed her Ts with precision.
Then it isn't.

I flip to the beginning. The first entries are unremarkable. *Arrived at Bly. Beautiful house. The twins are delightful.* The handwriting is steady. The observations are measured. A professional woman documenting her professional life.
I skip ahead. The paper is thicker here — she pressed harder.
*Day 47: They moved my medication. I'm certain I left it on the nightstand. Found it in the kitchen. Mrs. Grose says I'm misremembering. Am I?*
*Day 52: Miles asked if I ever think about dying. He's seven. Who taught him that word?*
*Day 61: I heard humming in Flora's room at 3 AM. Flora was asleep. Her lips weren't moving. The humming was coming from somewhere else in the room.*
*Day 74: I found mud on the hem of my dress. I haven't been outside. The lake mud has a particular smell — sulfurous, organic. This is that mud.*
*Day 89: Miles told me the woman in the lake is my fault. He said it the way children say things they've heard adults say. Parroting. He wasn't upset. He was delivering information.*
My throat is tight. I keep reading.
*Day 91: Mrs. Grose found me crying in the kitchen. She put her hand on my shoulder. She said, "Some houses don't let you leave, dear." Then she laughed like it was a joke. Her hand was very heavy.*
*Day 103: I'm not the governess. I'm the content. They're watching me. Not the children. Something else. Something that watches through the children.*

The loops have collapsed. The Gs are angular. The words tilt right, then left, as if the page moved while she wrote. I turn to the next entry and the handwriting changes entirely — cramped, urgent, letters crushed together like people in a lifeboat.

*I tried to leave. The car wouldn't start. Mrs. Grose was in the kitchen, kneading bread. She didn't look up. She said, "Leaving so soon? The road's awful at night." How did she know I was in the drive? I didn't make a sound.*

*Day 120: I've started packing. I can't finish. Every time I go to close the suitcase, I find something of mine has been moved back to my room. A sock. A book. As if the house is unpacking me while I pack.*
I turn the page. Blank. I turn another. Blank. Blank. Then the last page with writing on it, and the ink is different — darker, pressed so hard the pen almost tore the paper:
*SHE KNOWS SHE KNOWS SHE KNOWS SHE KNOWS SHE KNOWS SHE KNOWS SHE KNOWS SHE KNOWS*
A line of it. Then below, smaller:
*She knows I know she knows.*

I close the book. My hands are shaking. Not from fear — from recognition. This is evidence. This is a paper trail. This is a woman documenting her own unravelling, or her own correct interpretation of something terrible, and either way, she is gone and her suitcase is still here, half-packed, waiting for a return that didn't happen.

I put the book in my jumper pocket. I close the suitcase. I turn off my phone's flashlight and stand in the absolute dark of the storage closet and I breathe. In. Out. The bleach smell is stronger now, or I'm more aware of it. Under it: the lake. That sulfurous, organic smell Jessel wrote about. Or am I imagining it?
I walk back to my room. The corridor feels longer in the dark. Every shadow has edges I didn't notice before.
Morning arrives gray and damp. The windows weep condensation. I go downstairs and find Mrs. Grose in the kitchen.

She's kneading bread. The dough is pale and elastic under her hands. Her forearms flex with each push. Fold, press, turn. Fold, press, turn. The rhythm is mechanical, industrial. This is not baking. This is work, and the dough is receiving the brunt of something that has nowhere else to go.
"Good morning, miss." She doesn't look up. "Tea's in the pot."
"Thank you." I pour myself a cup. The ceramic is thick, old, chip on the handle. I wrap my hands around it for warmth. "Mrs. Grose, I was wondering about Miss Jessel."
The dough receives a particularly aggressive push. Flour puffs into the air. "Miss Jessel?"
"The previous governess. I found her suitcase in the storage closet. It seemed — unfinished."
A pause. One turn of the dough. Then another. "That closet shouldn't have been open."
"It wasn't locked."
Another push. Harder. The wooden board creaks beneath the dough. "Things don't get locked properly around here. House is too old. Everything's shifted."
"Where did Miss Jessel go?"

"Found the position wasn't to her liking." The phrase comes out rehearsed. Practiced. The same words, the same cadence, as if she's answered this question before and has settled on the official version.
"That's an interesting way to put it."

"Is it?" Fold. Press. Turn. Mrs. Grose's hands are red from the flour and the pressure. Her knuckles whiten with each push. The dough must be long past kneading by now. She's pummeling it. The shape doesn't matter anymore — it's being punished, not formed.
"Most people would say she quit. Or she was let go. 'Wasn't to her liking' sounds like —" I stop myself. I'm pushing. I can hear myself pushing.

"Sounds like what, miss?" She looks at me now. Her face is neutral, pleasant, the face of a woman who has worked in service her whole life and knows how to arrange her features into a mask of professional calm. But her hands haven't stopped. The dough is being destroyed. She's pressing the life out of it.
"Sounds like you're choosing your words carefully."
The slightest pause. Then she returns to the bread. "I always choose my words carefully, miss. You should try it."
The kitchen is quiet except for the sound of dough being worked. The kettle clicks off. Somewhere in the house, a pipe groans. Mrs. Grose's hands push, push, push. I take my tea and leave.

I spend the afternoon in my room with Jessel's journal. I read it front to back this time. I photograph every page with my phone. I back up the photos to the cloud. If this house has taught Jessel anything, it's that objects can be moved. Documents can disappear. The cloud is somewhere else. The cloud doesn't live at Bly.

The entries paint a clear arc: confident arrival, creeping doubt, mounting dread, frantic certainty. Classic. Textbook. Every breakdown follows the same shape. I know this because I wrote my dissertation on institutional gaslighting. I know the presentation. I know how it looks when someone is being made to distrust their own senses, and I know how it looks when someone is genuinely unwell, and the tragedy is that they look identical from the outside, which is the whole point, which is why the method works.

Day 47: they moved her medication. Or she misplaced it. Day 61: humming from an empty room. Or auditory misperception, the brain filling in patterns where none exist. Day 89: Miles, parroting. Or Miles, reporting. Day 120: the suitcase unpacking itself. Or Jessel unpacking it in her sleep, stress and sleepwalking, documented correlation.
I can explain every entry. That's what frightens me. I can explain all of it, and my explanations don't make me feel any better.
I fall asleep reading. When I wake, the room is dark and the phone says 3:02 AM and someone is humming.
The sound is close. Not in the wall. Not through the floor. In the room.
I sit up. My breath comes fast and shallow. The humming is low, tuneless, the way you hum when you're not aware you're doing it. A melody that isn't a melody. Sound for the sake of sound.
Flora stands at the foot of my bed.

Her eyes are closed. Her hair is loose around her shoulders, dark against the white of her nightgown. She sways, slightly, like a plant in a draft. Her lips are parted. The humming comes from her throat, soft and even, the sound of someone in a trance, or performing one.
"Flora." My voice is a rasp. "Flora, wake up."
Her eyes open. They find me immediately. There is nothing sleepwalker-like about them. She is present. She is here. She looks at me the way a doctor looks at a patient — with calm, clinical interest.
"I was talking to Miss J," she says.
My heart is a fist hitting the inside of my ribs. "But Miss Jessel left."
Flora tilts her head. The swaying stops. "Did she?" A beat. "I don't think she wanted to go."
The room is cold. When did it get so cold? The window is closed. The radiator is ticking, doing its work, and still the air is winter.
"Flora, go back to bed."

"She stands by the lake," Flora says, and her voice has dropped into a register that doesn't belong to a seven-year-old. It's not deeper — it's flatter. Emptier. A voice reading off a script it doesn't understand. "She looks at the house. She doesn't come in. She can't come in unless someone invites her."
"Go back to bed. Now."

Flora blinks. The flatness recedes. She's a child again, small in her nightgown, standing in a dark room that smells like someone else's fear. "Goodnight, Maeve." She turns and pads to the door. Her bare feet make no sound on the floor.
The door closes. The humming doesn't resume. I sit in bed and I listen to myself breathe and I count my heartbeats and I wait for whatever comes next.
Nothing comes next. The house is silent. The radiator ticks. The water stain on the ceiling is still a continent that doesn't exist.

Back in my room. Same bed. Same ceiling. Same weight in my jumper pocket — Jessel's journal, or my photograph of it, the evidence that something happened here before me, that I am following a path someone else walked and didn't walk back from.
I sit on the edge of the bed and I calculate.

Option A: Bly is haunted. The twins are conduits for dead people. Jessel experienced the same phenomena and it destroyed her. The house feeds on its inhabitants. The children are accessories, or victims, or both. Under this option, I am in danger.

Option B: The twins are performing. They're children. Children test boundaries. Children lie, manipulate, say the creepiest possible things because they've learned, probably from the internet, what gets a reaction. Jessel was unwell — the journal is evidence of that. She projected her illness onto her environment. The children picked up on her fear and amplified it. Under this option, I am the audience for an elaborate game.

Option C: Both. The twins are performing, and something at Bly is real, and the two things are feeding each other in a loop I can't see the edges of. Under this option, I am in a house that contains both a mundane threat and an inexplicable one, and I can't tell which one is reaching for me at any given moment.
None of these options are good.

I open my suitcase. Under the clothes, in a pocket I haven't touched since I packed it in London: the satellite phone. Heavy, matte black, built for places with no signal, which Bly most certainly is. The Wi-Fi is Mrs. Grose's domain — she controls the router, she knows what comes and goes. The satellite phone doesn't touch her network. The satellite phone talks to the sky.
I turn it on. The screen glows blue in the dark room. I have signal. Three bars. The interface is clunky, designed for function over form. I open the browser. Slow. Latency from space. But it works.

I navigate to Reddit. The page loads in chunks, text first, then images. r/NoSleep. I've read it for years. I know the rules: everything is true here. The stories are fiction, but you treat them as fact. A performance of belief. A collaborative hallucination where the scariest thing is never the ghost — it's the narrator, alone, typing into the void, trying to make someone believe them.
The cursor blinks in the title field.

If I post, I commit to a version. I name what's happening to me. I label it: *haunted*, or *gaslit*, or *losing my mind*. Once it's written, it becomes the narrative. And narratives can be used against you. I know this. I've seen it happen. The woman who reports harassment and becomes "the harassment woman" forever. The student who files a complaint and becomes "the problem." You write your story and it belongs to everyone but you.
But.

The internet doesn't forget. I know this too. The internet is the worst thing that ever happened to privacy, to reputation, to the human right to be young and stupid and move on. It's also the best thing that ever happened to evidence. Things posted exist. Things written in a journal on a shelf in a storage closet can be moved, lost, burned. Things uploaded to a server in Palo Alto have timestamps. Have witnesses.

If something happens to me — whatever "something" means, whatever I'm not yet willing to name — there will be a record. Someone will be able to trace the shape of it. Jessel had her journal, and Jessel is gone, and the journal was sitting in a closet where nobody would have found it if I hadn't been insomnia-walking at three in the morning. Jessel didn't have the cloud. Jessel didn't have a satellite phone. Jessel didn't have r/NoSleep.
I type a title. My thumb hovers over Submit.
*My pupils are haunting me and I think the house is helping*
No. Delete. Too specific. Too committed.
*I'm a governess at a house where the last governess left something behind*
Closer. Still not right.
*Day 3 at Bly Manor: the children keep mentioning dead people and I found the previous governess's journal and I don't know what I'm dealing with*
My thumb is still hovering. The screen is warm against my face. Outside, the wind moves through the trees. The house settles. A pipe groans.

If I post, I'm asking someone to believe me. And if nobody believes me, then I'm just another story on r/NoSleep, fiction by convention, dismissed by design. Another unreliable narrator typing into the void.
But if I don't post, and something happens, then the only record of what I experienced is inside this house, and this house eats its records.
My thumb hovers.
The Submit button pulses gently, waiting.
